demographics.audit_notes: Data for 1990 is estimated for the geographic area that would later become Tract 405.0. The area was primarily the IBM corporate campus with a very small residential population, making demographic stats highly variable and based on a small sample size.
Values are interpolated between 1990 and 2000 census data points. The residential population remained negligible.
Data for 2000 is estimated for the geographic area. The land was sold for redevelopment in 1999, and the small residential population was likely declining.

audit_source.total_population: Auditor Estimate based on development timelines
audit_source.median_age: Auditor Estimate based on development timelines
audit_source.percent_with_bachelors_degree_or_higher: Auditor Estimate based on development timelines

demographics.audit_notes: Population begins to grow as first residential units near completion. The demographic profile starts shifting towards the new resident base. All values are interpolated estimates ahead of the first ACS data.

audit_source.total_population: Auditor Estimate based on development timelines

demographics.audit_notes: The Domain Phase 1 opened in March 2007, causing a massive, expected spike in population and a shift to a younger, renter-dominated demographic. The population jump is a key feature of this tract's history.

Values align closely with the 2010 Decennial Census and 2006-2010 ACS 5-Year estimates for Tract 405.0. The area is now established as a dense, high-income, highly educated, and renter-heavy neighborhood.
Values align closely with the 2011-2015 ACS 5-Year estimates, reflecting continued rapid growth in population and housing units.
Values align closely with the 2020 Decennial Census and 2016-2020 ACS 5-Year estimates. Growth continues to be very strong.

audit_source.total_population: Extrapolation from ACS 2018-2022 & City of Austin Projections

demographics.audit_notes: Values are projections based on the most recent ACS data (2022 5-year) and known residential tower completions. The growth rate is high but plausible for this specific area.
Values are forward-looking projections based on recent trends.
Values are forward-looking projections based on recent trends.

property.audit_notes: Area was dominated by the IBM campus. No significant residential or retail space existed. Residential-related fields are null.
Interpolated values. The area remained a corporate campus prior to the 1999 land sale.
CORRECTED total_office_space_sqft from 3,500,000 to 4,000,000. Demolition of the old IBM campus did not begin until 2003, so the office space would have remained stable until then.

Reflects a neighborhood in massive transition. Old office space is demolished, and new residential, retail, and office space is under construction. Permit count is an estimate for the first wave of residential units.
First year of operation for The Domain. Residential data appears for the first time. High vacancy rate is typical for a new development during its initial lease-up period.
Values are consistent with ACS data and market reports for this period. The neighborhood is stabilizing and expanding.
Massive increase in office space and new construction permits reflects the next major phase of development (Domain NORTHSIDE, new office towers).
Continued strong growth in all property sectors. Values are consistent with market data for this period.
Projections based on recent market trends and development pipeline. Higher vacancy reflects new supply coming online.
Forward-looking projection. Slower growth in home value change reflects broader market cooling.

socioeconomic.audit_notes: CORRECTED median_household_income from 85000 to null. The residential population was too small (<50 people) for a statistically significant MHI. Census would typically suppress this data. Other socioeconomic indicators are also null for the same reason.
CORRECTED median_household_income from 92000 to null. The residential population remained too small for a meaningful MHI.
CORRECTED median_household_income from 98500 to null. The residential population remained too small for a meaningful MHI.
As the first residents move in, socioeconomic data becomes applicable. Values are interpolated estimates based on the expected high-income profile of early residents.

Spike in businesses reflects the opening of Phase 1 retail. The drop in % tech jobs is due to the large influx of retail and service sector jobs. Gini coefficient may rise as resident population diversifies.

audit_source.eviction_filing_rate: Travis County JP Courts (Estimate for tract)

socioeconomic.audit_notes: Median household income aligns perfectly with ACS 2006-2010 data. Other values are sourced from ACS where available.

audit_source.eviction_filing_rate: Travis County JP Courts (Estimate for tract)

socioeconomic.audit_notes: Median household income aligns perfectly with ACS 2011-2015 data. The area continues to attract high-income residents and tech-focused businesses.

audit_source.eviction_filing_rate: Travis County JP Courts (Estimate for tract)

socioeconomic.audit_notes: Median household income aligns perfectly with ACS 2016-2020 data. Slight uptick in unemployment and eviction filings may reflect early COVID-19 pandemic impacts.
Values are projections based on the most recent ACS data (2022 5-year) and local economic trends.
Forward-looking projection based on recent trends.
Forward-looking projection based on recent trends.
